RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-4-oxo-7-(4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazin-1-yl)-1-[3-(4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]quinoline-3-carboxylicacid This is a complex quinolone-3-carboxylic acid derivative, whose backbone is also a core structure for quinolone antibiotics. The compounds most prominent feature is that it carries two large 4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazine substituents: one linked at position 7 of the quinoline ring, and the other linked via a three-carbon propylene chain at the N1 position. This double, polyheterocyclic, highly basic substitution pattern is typical of designs for multi-target drugs or agents with enhanced membrane permeability. The pyridine-piperazine moiety is frequently used in designing Central Nervous System (CNS) drugs. Therefore, the structure of this compound suggests it may be studied as a novel antibacterial agent, particularly against resistant strains, or as a ligand for dopamine/serotonin receptors.

Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-1-methyl-4-oxo-7-(4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazin-1-yl)quinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as WM5 (1-methyl-7-[4-(2-pyridyl)-1-piperazinyl]-6-amino-4-oxo-1,4-dihydroquinoline-3-carboxylic acid), this compound is a structural analog of quinolone antibiotics (like Ofloxacin), originally optimized as a quinolone antibacterial but subsequently found to act as a high-affinity ligand for the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulge, it retains the canonical 4-oxo-3-carboxylate warhead, replaces the customary C-6 fluorine with an unsubstituted amino group and carries a small N-1 methyl and a bulky 4-(2-pyridyl)-1-piperazine at C-7, can bind TAR RNA, block Tat-activated transcription, suppress HIV-1 replication in both acutely and chronically infected T-cell lines without affecting reverse transcriptase, integrase or protease activities, while esterification of its carboxylate (WM5E) abolishes both RNA binding and antiviral efficacy, the compound displays low micromolar cytotoxicity and is essentially inactive against HSV-1, underscoring its selectivity for HIV-1 and establishing it as a promising lead for a new class of transcription-targeted antiretrovirals that act by high-affinity recognition of viral RNA rather than classical hosttopoisomerase inhibition, and is likely a quinolone derivative designed to explore new antibacterial or antiproliferative activity.

RNA information: This RNA forms a well-ordered binding pocket through interlocking structural motifs . In the presence of Mg²+, it can bind theophylline with high affinity  and exhibits molecular discrimination specificity towards caffeine.
Small_molecule_information: 1,3,7-trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Caffeine, structurally similar to theophylline but with an extra methyl group at the N7 position, lacks the bronchodilator function of theophylline and is structurally distinct from theophylline due to this N-7 methyl group; it functions as a competitor for theophylline-binding RNA aptamers, binds theophylline-binding RNA far weaker, its N7 methyl disrupts key H-bonds and causes steric repulsion, failing to form the RNAs core base triples and stacking motifs needed for stable binding, explaining RNAs molecular discrimination, and while no direct diseases are associated with it, its structural similarity to theophylline may interfere with diagnostic assays for theophylline-treated conditions like asthma and bronchitis.

Small_molecule_information: 1,3-dimethyl-7,9-dihydro-1H-purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione also referred to as 1,3-Dimethyluric acid is an oxopurine metabolite bearing carbonyls at C-2, C-6 and C-8 and methyl groups at N-1 and N-3, it is a theophylline derivative that represents the terminal oxidation product of theophylline in humans, differing from theophylline by an additional 8-position keto group and an N9 proton; introduction of the C-8 carbonyl forces the ring into the 7,9-dihydro-1H-purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione tautomer, enlarges the -system and creates a steric/electrostatic clash with the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket, no specific biological function is mentioned; its key role is in studying RNA-ligand binding, it binds theophylline-binding RNA with much lower affinity than theophylline, its N9 proton disrupts the N9-U24 H-bond, and the 8-keto group causes steric interference which together with the steric/electrostatic clash with the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket fails to stabilize RNAs core base triples, demonstrating that an unsubstituted C-8 position is essential for high-affinity recognition by the evolved RNA sensor, thus explaining the reduced binding.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA

Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

Small_molecule_information: naphthalen-2-ol, also referred to as 2-Naphthol (commonly known as -Naphthol), is a crucial fundamental aromatic compound, and 2-Naphthol is a simple aromatic compound characterized by a hydroxyl group attached to a naphthalene ring, it consists of a naphthalene scaffold fused from two benzene rings with a hydroxyl group attached at the 2-position, -Naphthol is a weakly acidic white solid with a characteristic phenolic odor, which is extensively used in the chemical industry, in reality, the primary use of -Naphthol is as a key intermediate in the synthesis of various azo dyes, which are widely used for coloring textiles, paper, and leather, additionally, it is used as a raw material for synthesizing antioxidants, pesticides, preservatives, and other pharmaceuticals and fragrances, due to its structural similarity to natural phenolic substances, it and its derivatives also possess some antimicrobial and antifungal activity, the figure shows that 2-naphthol has a certain binding affinity but does not induce gene expression, indicating that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA. -RNA

Small_molecule_information: 1H-indol-6-ol, also referred to as 6-hydroxyindole, is a hydroxyl derivative of the indole scaffold. Structurally, it is an azaaromatic molecule with an indole core (fused benzene and pyrrole rings) and a hydroxyl group at the 6-position, exhibiting planar and hydrophobic characteristics typical of azaaromatics; the indole ring (fused from a benzene ring and a pyrrole ring) is an extremely important class of heterocycles in nature, being the core structure of tryptophan and many neurotransmitters (such as Serotonin), thus having great significance in neuroscience and drug development. 6-hydroxyindole is a metabolic precursor or related structure to the pineal gland hormone Melatonin, playing a role in in vivo metabolism, and while the document focuses on azaaromatics binding yjdF riboswitches, no info on this compounds riboswitch binding affinity or gene expression effects is given; in reality, such compounds are often used as synthetic building blocks for neuroactive drugs, for developing selective serotonin receptor agonists or antagonists, and for studying drugs for sleep regulation, mood disorders, and migraines.

Small_molecule_information: quinolin-6-ol, also referred to as 6-hydroxyquinoline , is one of the simple hydroxyl derivatives of the quinoline scaffold, with the hydroxyl group located at the 6-position on the benzene ring part; structurally, it is an azaaromatic compound with a quinoline core (fused benzene-pyridine ring) and a hydroxyl group at the 6-position, featuring planar, hydrophobic properties. The presence of the quinoline scaffold endows the molecule with a good conjugated system and potential medicinal activity. The 6-hydroxyl substituent is a hydrogen bond donor/acceptor that can influence the molecules acid-base properties, solubility, and interaction patterns with biological targets; functionally, as an azaaromatic analog, it might potentially interact with yjdF riboswitches (per the documents focus) but lacks reported binding affinity or gene expression activation for such riboswitches in the given content. In reality, quinolinol derivatives are typically explored as various bioactive compounds, including antimicrobial agents, antimalarials (such as quinine, chloroquine), or metal chelating agents. 6-hydroxyquinoline can form stable five- or six-membered rings with metal ions, making it potentially useful as a biometal probe or as a ligand in material science.

Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-2-chloro-1H-purin-6-one, also referred to as 2-chloroinosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphate (2-Cl-cIMP), is a complex nucleotide analog with the molecular formula C10H10ClN4O8P, it has a cyclic nucleotide structure: an inosine base with a 2-chloro substitution, linked to a ribose ring forming a 3,5-cyclic monophosphate ester, its nucleobase is a derivative of 2-chloroguanine (2-chloro-1H-purin-6-one), and the sugar moiety is a specialized structure containing a cyclic phosphate ester, this cyclic phosphate structure mimics intracellular second messengers like cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP), the 2-chloro substituent is often used to enhance the lipophilicity and prevent metabolic deactivation of purine-based drugs, functionally, as a modified cyclic nucleotide, it may modulate cyclic nucleotide-dependent signaling pathways (e.g., by interacting with cyclic nucleotide-gated channels or kinases), though specific roles (e.g., in riboswitch binding or bacterial signaling) are unreported in relevant documents, as a nucleotide analog, this compound is designed to interfere with intracellular nucleic acid metabolism and signal transduction pathways, its potential activities include acting as an antiviral or anti-cancer drug by inhibiting viral or cellular DNA/RNA polymerases, or by functioning as a cell apoptosis inducer.

Small_molecule_information: Pyridine-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Nicotinic acid or one form of Vitamin B3, has the formula C6H5NO2; it is a planar azaaromatic molecule, with its structure characterized by a pyridine ring (the azaaromatic core) and a carboxyl group (-COOH) attached at the 3-position of the pyridine ring. Nicotinic acid is an essential human vitamin that binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and it serves as a precursor for the vital coenzymes Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD) and Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide Phosphate (NADP) (key redox coenzymes in metabolism), which are critical in numerous cellular oxidation-reduction reactions, energy metabolism, DNA repair, and cell signaling. In real-world medicine, besides preventing and treating B3 deficiency diseases like pellagra (a niacin deficiency disorder), Nicotinic acid is widely used at pharmacological doses (higher than the recommended daily allowance) as a lipid-modifying agent: it effectively lowers levels of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglycerides, and significantly increases high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) levels, thus contributing to cardiovascular disease prevention, though side effects like flushing must be managed, and it also plays core roles in regulating lipid levels.

Small_molecule_information: (E)-3-phenylprop-2-enoic acid, also referred to as Cinnamic acid, is an aromatic unsaturated carboxylic acid with the formula C9H8O2; it is an unsaturated fatty acid characterized by a benzene ring (i.e., phenyl group) connected to a vinyl group and a carboxyl group, with its structure specifically consisting of a phenyl group connected to a carboxylic acid group via a trans (E) double bond. Cinnamic acid occurs naturally in plants, particularly in cinnamon bark and storax, and acts as an important biosynthetic precursor in plants for various secondary metabolites like flavonoids, lignin, and coumarins. In practical applications, Cinnamic acid and its esters are known for their pleasant, characteristic aroma, making them widely used in the flavoring, perfume, and food industries. Furthermore, its conjugated structure allows it to absorb UV radiation, leading to its use as an ingredient in sunscreens to protect the skin from UV damage. It and its derivatives are also investigated for certain antimicrobial, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory activities. In terms of its interaction with 108yjdF RNA, Cinnamic acid does not have a strong binding affinity and does not induce gene expression, implying that it may not be an effective ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2-[[4-[(2-amino-4-oxo-3H-pteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid is the chemical structure for Folic Acid (FA), also known as Vitamin B9 and also referred to as a non-azaaromatic B-vitamin (B9), which is structured with an oxidized pteridine ring, para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), and a glutamic acid moietyunlike its reduced form tetrahydrofolate (THF), and it lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; folic acid is a vital B vitamin required for cell division, DNA synthesis, and red blood cell maturation, it must be biologically activated by reduction to tetrahydrofolate (THF) by the enzyme DHFR, with its core role being a dietary precursor that needs to be converted to THF to act as a one-carbon carrier for nucleotide synthesis and cell division, which is critical for fetal development and red blood cell production; clinically, the compound is widely used as a nutritional supplement, especially for pregnant women to prevent fetal neural tube defects, and for treating megaloblastic anemia caused by folate deficiency, and many anticancer drugs (e.g., methotrexate) are competitive inhibitors of this molecules activation pathway.

Small_molecule_information: 7H-purine-2,6-diamine, also referred to as 2,6-diaminopurine (DAP), is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound and a planar aromatic base with an additional amino group at the C2 position of adenine, a purine nucleobase where both positions 2 and 6 of the purine ring are substituted with amino groups, molecular formula C5H6N6. In terms of real-world activity, DAP is an important non-natural nucleic acid base with significant biological and pharmacological relevance; through its extended hydrogen bond donor, it forms a denser network with the riboswitch core pocket, and its complex lifetime lasts for several minutes, while it can also be incorporated into the DNA strand, where it forms a more stable three-hydrogen-bond pair with Thymine (T) than the natural two-hydrogen-bond A-T pair, a stronger pairing property that is highly useful in designing DNA oligonucleotide probes and PCR primers with enhanced stability. Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-1H-pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Cytosine, is one of the four fundamental nucleobases constituting nucleic acids (DNA and RNA), structured as a non-planar pyrimidine nucleobase with a six-membered heterocyclic ring containing two nitrogen atoms, a keto group at position 2 and an amino group at position 4 (also described as an amino group at position 6 in its standard nomenclature), molecular formula C4H5N3O, and it lacks planar azaaromatic features. In medicine, many synthetic analogs of Cytosine, such as Cytarabine, have been developed as important anticancer drugs for treating acute leukemia, which exert their cytotoxic effects by disrupting normal Cytosine metabolism and DNA replication.

Small_molecule_information: 7,8-dimethyl-1H-benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as Lumichrome, is a nitrogen-containing tetracyclic aromatic heterocyclic compound and a natural FMN degradation product and azaaromatic-like compound that binds to yjdF motif RNAs (azaaromatic riboswitches) as determined by in-line probing, with binding inducing structural changes in the RNA similar to other azaaromatic ligands, however, the article does not mention it triggering riboswitch-mediated gene expression, and it is not considered the natural ligand of these riboswitches, its structure is based on the benzo[g]pteridine core, formed by the fusion of a benzene ring and a pteridine ring, with 7,8-dimethyl substitution on the benzene portion and a 2,4-dione (imide) structure on the pteridine ring, the molecular formula is CHNO, this compound is the core chromophore flavin component of the vitamin Riboflavin, it has a planar conjugated system and is photosensitive, in terms of biological function, as the parent core of natural riboflavin (Vitamin B2), its derivatives play an essential role in electron transfer within the respiratory chain and energy metabolism.

Small_molecule_information: 1-[10-(4-amino-2-methylquinolin-1-ium-1-yl)decyl]-2-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-amine, also referred to as dequalinium, is a bis-quaternary ammonium salt compound where two quinoline units are linked by a long carbon chain, with 4-amino-2-methylquinoline as the repeating unit: the two quinoline units are bridged at their quaternary N1 atoms by a decyl (decyl) carbon chain, making the molecule carry two positive charges, with a calculated molecular formula of CHN, and this nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, as it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure that are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine and chelerythrine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity with 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, while although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, dequalinium, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; such dicationic compounds, especially long-chain linked aromatic cations, are well-known disinfectants and antimicrobial agents, and also show high affinity for DNA/RNA.

Small_molecule_information: 2-acetyloxybenzoic acid, also referred to as acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin), is an aromatic carboxylate ester compound, with benzoic acid as its parent structure: the hydroxyl group at position 2 of benzoic acid is substituted by an acetyl group to form an ester bond, and this compound, with the molecular formula C9H8O4, is the chemical name for the well-known non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug Aspirin, which exhibits antipyretic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antiplatelet aggregation activities, while its structural features result in weak binding to the 108yjdF RNA and inability to induce riboswitch-mediated gene expression, indicating it is not an active ligand for this RNA.

Small_molecule_information: N-[(3R,4R,5S,6R)-2,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]acetamide, also referred to as N-acetyl-D-glucosamine (GlcNAc), is a typical amino sugar derivative, with the oxane ring as its core and the stereoconfiguration (3R,4R,5S,6R), containing multiple hydroxyl groups and a hydroxymethyl group, and the amino group at position 3 substituted with an acetamide group; it functions as an essential monomer for constructing biological macromolecules such as chitin, peptidoglycan, and glycoproteins, playing key roles in cell recognition, immune response, and biosynthesis, while lacking planar hydrophobic azaaromatic features and therefore not binding tightly to the yjdF motif RNA nor triggering the corresponding riboswitch-mediated gene regulation in Bacillus subtilis

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[(4R)-4-[(3R,5R,8R,9S,10S,12S,13R,14S,17R)-3,12-dihydroxy-10,13-dimethyl-2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,11,12,14,15,16,17-tetradecahydro-1H-cyclopenta[a]phenanthren-17-yl]pentanoyl]amino]ethanesulfonic acid, also referred to as Taurodeoxycholic acid, is a non-azaaromatic taurine-conjugated bile acid with a steroid nucleus (four fused cycloalkane rings), a side chain linked to taurine, and hydroxyl groups at specific positions that lacks planar hydrophobic azaaromatic moieties, an essential component of bile secretion synthesized in the liver; it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and its physiological function is to emulsify dietary fats, promote the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins, regulate cholesterol metabolism and aid fat digestion and absorption in the gut (a role unrelated to azaaromatic sensing), while in clinical practice, taurine-conjugated bile salts are used to dissolve cholesterol gallstones and treat certain cholestatic liver diseases, playing a critical role in maintaining enterohepatic circulation.

RNA information: The 161 yjdF RNA from B. subtilis, part of the azaaromatic riboswitch class, has a four-stem (P1-P4) structure with conserved regions. It binds diverse azaaromatics  via a single site, triggering similar structural changes. Ligand binding activates gene expression by unblocking the ribosome-binding site, aiding potential detox. It doesn’t bind phenolic acids/bile salts well and differs from FMN riboswitches in ligand specificity.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[3-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-2-methoxyphenoxy]propoxy]-3-methoxyphenyl]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole, also referred to as DIMP, is a molecule characterized by the imidazolin (4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole) functional group, lacking the pentacyclic scaffold, which connects two methyl-imidazoline aromatic rings with a flexible propane chain (or via a propoxy bridge) to form a structure consisting of two imidazolin-substituted phenol derivatives linked by the aforementioned chain/bridge, endowing the molecule with a moderate degree of rotational freedom, enabling it to embrace the RNA main groove and induce local helical deformation; its electron-rich imidazoline nitrogen can form a hydrogen bond network with RNA 2-OH and base edge groups, interfering with the coordination of metal ions at the active site, causing instability of the catalytic core and thereby inhibiting the splicing reaction, while the imidazolin groups of the molecule are strongly basic and easily protonated at physiological conditions to form cations, so this molecule is anticipated to be dicationic, and as imidazolin derivatives are common in medicinal chemistry, notably in 2-adrenergic receptor agonists (like the antihypertensive drug clonidine), in practical applications, this type of structure may be explored as a small molecule DNA/RNA binder or as a compound with vasoactive properties.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-3,7-dihydropurine-2,6-dione (also referred to as 1-methylxanthine) is a theophylline derivative that lacks the N3 methyl group of theophylline; it is a monomethylxanthine in which a methyl group is attached to N-1 and the imidazole ring is protonated at N-7, occurs naturally as a minor purine alkaloid and has been identified as a mouse metabolite. It has no mentioned specific biological function; its key relevance lies in binding theophylline-binding RNA. Compared to theophylline, the absence of the N-3 methyl and the presence of only a single N-1 methyl reduce its complementarity to the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket, making it unable to fit into the hydrophobic pocket formed by RNAs C8, A28, and U24, though it still relies on core RNA bases (C22, U24) for weak binding, which explains its lower binding affinity, and both the N-3 methyl and the N-7 hydrogen-bond donor are critical for high-affinity recognition by the evolved RNA aptamer.

Small_molecule_information: 3-methyl-7H-purine-2,6-dione, also referred to as 3-Methylxanthine, is one of the core structures of Theobromine; this compound is a monomethylxanthine that carries a methyl group at N-3 and exists mainly as the 3-methyl-9H-xanthine tautomer (proton on N-9), occurs naturally as a purine catabolite and is recognized as a mammalian metabolite, retains the N-3 methyl substituent which is one of the key contacts within the hydrophobic pocket of the TCT8-4 RNA aptamer so its affinity for theophylline-binding RNA is only modestly reduced while the loss of the N-7 hydrogen-bond donor still prevents high-affinity binding, underscoring the RNAs evolved requirement for both the N-3 methyl and the N-7 proton of theophylline, and Theobromine is a mild diuretic and myocardial stimulant found in chocolate, often used for bronchodilation.

Small_molecule_information: 1,3-dimethyl-7,9-dihydro-1H-purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione also referred to as 1,3-Dimethyluric acid is an oxopurine metabolite bearing carbonyls at C-2, C-6 and C-8 and methyl groups at N-1 and N-3, it is a theophylline derivative that represents the terminal oxidation product of theophylline in humans, differing from theophylline by an additional 8-position keto group and an N9 proton; introduction of the C-8 carbonyl forces the ring into the 7,9-dihydro-1H-purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione tautomer, enlarges the -system and creates a steric/electrostatic clash with the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket, no specific biological function is mentioned; its key role is in studying RNA-ligand binding, it binds theophylline-binding RNA with much lower affinity than theophylline, its N9 proton disrupts the N9-U24 H-bond, and the 8-keto group causes steric interference which together with the steric/electrostatic clash with the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket fails to stabilize RNAs core base triples, demonstrating that an unsubstituted C-8 position is essential for high-affinity recognition by the evolved RNA sensor, thus explaining the reduced binding.

RNA information: H19 is an RNA aptamer with a stem-loop structure predicted by Mulfold, specifically binding Hoechst 33258. It synergizes with H10 to enhance translation inhibition in vitro and gene expression regulation in cells. While not directly linked to diseases, its molecular binding property holds potential in cancer research, supporting investigations into DNA - associated pathological processes.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenol, also referred to as Hoechst 33258 (H33258), is a fluorescent dye with morpholine rings, benzimidazole moieties, and methylated benzene rings, binding to DNAs minor groove. It enables fluorescent imaging and acts as a ligand for RNA aptamers to regulate gene expression. While not a drug, it supports research in cancer and other DNA-associated pathological processes, aiding investigations into disease mechanisms. 4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenol This compound is a bis-benzimidazole derivative featuring a rigid, extended conjugated system formed by two linked benzimidazole units. The molecule is substituted with a basic N-methylpiperazinyl group on one end and a phenolic hydroxyl group on the other. Due to its structural characteristics, this compound is often studied as a DNA minor groove binder. By binding to the minor groove of DNA, it interferes with DNA replication and transcription, thus exhibiting potential anticancer activity.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 6,7-dimethoxy-2-(4-methylsulfonylpiperazin-1-yl)quinazolin-4-amine, also referred to as Compound 5, is a compound where the piperazine ring is substituted with a methylsulfonyl group, and Compound 5 is a DPQ analogue where the piperazine secondary amine is replaced with a methylsulfonyl group, the sulfonyl group is a strong electron-withdrawing, highly polar group that significantly impacts the basicity of the piperazine nitrogen, in practical applications, sulfonamide/sulfonyl structures are central to many antidiabetic drugs (sulfonylureas), diuretics, and carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, for this compound, the methylsulfonyl group makes it a highly polar 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist, it binds to the influenza RNA promoter, causing NMR spectral changes similar to compound 1, however, it showed no antiviral activity and no cytotoxicity in cellular assays, this high polarity might limit its ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, making it a more peripherally selective antihypertensive agent, thus reducing CNS side effects.

Small_molecule_information: [4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-thiophen-2-ylmethanone, also referred to as Compound 15, is a compound where the acyl side chain here is a thiophen-2-carbonyl (thiophen-2-ylmethanone) group, and Compound 15 is a DPQ (compound 1) derivative modified on the piperazines secondary amine (Rthiophene), synthesized via one-step coupling, thiophene, a sulfur-containing five-membered aromatic heterocycle, is a common bioisostere for the benzene ring, often used to improve metabolic stability and maintain activity, thiophene scaffolds are widely present in antithrombotic agents and anti-inflammatory drugs, the compounds primary activity is still as an 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist, it binds the influenza A RNA promoter and has the lowest anti-influenza activity but shows cytotoxicity to MDCK-HA cells, the incorporation of thiophene is typically aimed at extending the drugs half-life and improving bioavailability, leading to a more consistent clinical effect in treating hypertension and urological conditions.

Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]butan-1-one, also referred to as compound 8 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with modifications on the piperazines secondary amine (RPr)), is a quinazoline derivative. Its structure is based on the quinazoline core (a benzene ring fused to a pyrimidine ring), with a piperazine ring attached at the 2-position. The 4-position of the piperazine is substituted with a butanoyl group, and the quinazoline ring has an amino group at the 4-position and dimethoxy groups at the 6 and 7 positions. This compound is a typical small-molecule heterocycle, synthesized via one-step coupling, which adopts a non-planar structure; the quinazoline core is aromatic, the piperazine ring adds flexibility, and the amino and methoxy groups can act as hydrogen bond donors and acceptors. In terms of biological function, quinazoline derivatives are an important class of drug scaffolds, widely known for activities such as antitumor, antihypertensive (1 receptor antagonism, e.g., Doxazosin), and anti-inflammatory effects, and this compound binds the influenza A RNA promoter similarly to compound 1, has improved anti-influenza activity and no significant cytotoxicity.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 24 (EBAB, Pentamidine), is an amidine linked by an alkoxy chain, where two amidine groups are connected by a relatively long pentyloxy ether chain, this molecule is an aromatic diamidine with benzamidine pharmacophore and features a 1,2-ethane/1,5-bis(4-amidinophenoxy)pentane scaffold and bisamidinium groups, its bis-amidine ether structure is highly significant in medicinal chemistry, exemplified by the well-known antiprotozoal drug Pentamidine which acts by binding to parasite DNA, it can also bind r(CUG)exps UU loops/5CUG/3GUC hairpins, displace MBNL1, correct DM1 splicing defects, act as an antibiotic adjuvant against multidrug-resistant bacteria, inhibit Class I intron self-splicing, and has low toxicity, the structural features of the molecule make it a candidate for a potential antiprotozoal or antifungal drug particularly against diseases like Pneumocystis pneumonia, and its activity is closely related to the length and flexibility of the linker chain, while also showing promise for DM1 and drug-resistant infections.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. Small_molecule_information: 2-chloro-5-(trifluoromethyl)benzenecarboximidamide, is a compound based on the carboximidamide (benzamidine) scaffold, substituted with a chlorine atom and a trifluoromethyl group on the benzene ring, with the formula C8H6ClF3N2. The imidamide group (C(NH)NH2) is typically protonated to a cation at physiological pH, making it a characteristic pharmacophore for DNA minor groove binders and protease inhibitors. It is studied for use as antiparasitic (e.g., against trypanosomiasis) and antibacterial agents. RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. RNA information: CA-uraA refers to a kind of uracil ribose switch from the genus Clostridium acetobutylicum. The structural features of this ribose switch include a specific RNA secondary structure, which is composed of one or more stem-ring regions organized around a three-way junction point, forming the binding site of uracil. The function of CA-uraA is to specifically recognize and bind to uracil through its aptamer region, thereby regulating gene expression. When uracil binds to the aptamer region, it causes a rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change can lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. This regulatory mechanism of the CA-uraA ribose switch enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular uracil concentration changes, thereby playing an important role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-[4-[N-(3-azidopropyl)carbamimidoyl]phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as AU-azide, is a highly functionalized probe molecule with the molecular formula C24H23N7O, and AU-azide is a derivative of Compound 2 with an azide group for conjugation; it exhibits even higher AU selectivity and is used to build multivalent compounds like dimers, its core is a planar diaryl-furan (biphenyl-furan) system, which provides hydrophobicity and - stacking ability, the molecule contains two key functional groups: carboximidamide and azido, the carboximidamide group (-C(NH)NH2) is typically positively charged at physiological pH, allowing it to interact strongly with negatively charged biomolecules like DNA; thus, amidine derivatives are often used as DNA minor groove binders with anti-tumor or antimicrobial activity, the azido group (-N3) is a prominent bio-orthogonal chemistry functional group, specifically a core component of Click Chemistry, which permits the molecule to be covalently linked to alkyne-containing partners under specific conditions, therefore, this compound is designed as a bifunctional molecular probe for target identification and bio-orthogonal chemical labeling.

RNA information: r(AUUCU)11: This RNA is an 11-repeat unit that models the pathogenic expansion found in the intron of the ATXN10 mRNA in SCA10. Its secondary structure is characterized by periodically repeating 5'AU/3'UA base pair steps, creating an extended, repetitive binding platform. It is the primary disease-relevant target in the study, and its key functional feature is its ability to be recognized with high affinity and positive cooperativity by the designed dimeric compound 2AU-2. Binding to this RNA disrupts its interaction with proteins like hnRNP K, which is the fundamental pathogenic mechanism. This disruption, demonstrated in cellular models, leads to the amelioration of disease phenotypes, including reduced nuclear foci and improved mitochondrial function.

RNA information: r(CUG)109 is a pathogenic RNA motif composed of 109 CUG repeats that folds into a stable, elongated hairpin structure containing periodic 1×1 UU internal loops. It functions as a toxic RNA by specifically binding to and sequestering muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, thereby disrupting its normal splicing regulation and contributing to the molecular pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Small molecules that selectively bind r(CUG)109 can displace MBNL1, restore aberrant splicing patterns, and alleviate disease-associated phenotypes in cellular models .
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]butyl]-N-methyl-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-K4NMeS, is a massive N-methylated oligopeptide-heterocycle conjugate, which is a rationally designed, modular small molecule that functions as a potent and selective inhibitor of the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA; its structure features two benzimidazole RNA-binding modules (H motifs) displayed on an N-methyl peptide backbone, with the structural core being a five-residue N-methylated (2S)-alanine chain where N-methylation is incorporated to improve metabolic stability against peptidases and enhance cell membrane penetration, and this oligopeptide backbone is connected via amide bonds to a complex bis-benzimidazole unit, a structural motif well-known as a high-affinity DNA minor groove binder, while the design of 2H-K4NMeS enables the molecule to bind multivalently to two adjacent 1x1 UU internal loops within the r(CUG)exp hairpin; RNA information: The r(CUG)₆ oligonucleotide is a key in vitro model used to study the pathogenic CUG repeat expansion. Its repetitive sequence forms a stable stem-loop structure characterized by canonical GC base pairs and non-canonical, dynamic UU mismatches. This unique structural motif is the primary binding site for both the Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein and the designed small molecule ligands. The RNA was synthetically labeled with a TAMRA fluorophore at its 5' end for binding assays. In these assays, ligand binding to the UU mismatches induces a structural change that quenches the TAMRA fluorescence, allowing the direct measurement of binding affinity (K_D), thus establishing r(CUG)₆ as a critical tool for quantifying ligand-RNA interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[3-[3-[3-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]propylamino]propylamino]propyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 9, is an acridine-triazine dimer that is the most potent dimeric ligand featuring an oligoamino linker, which is aqueous-soluble, cell-permeable and can effectively disperse nuclear foci in DM1 model cells, and it differs from 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide and 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide in key structural aspects: the acridine unit is substituted at position 4 (acridine-4-carboxamide), and the linker connecting the two acridine units is a polypropylamino chain containing three propylamino units, with the molecular formula C63H72N22O2, in terms of real-world activity, the position 4 substitution on the acridine and the introduction of a polyamine linker significantly alter the molecules spatial conformation and electrostatic interactions with DNA, the polyamine structure (like spermidine/spermine derivatives) is itself a strong binding ligand for the DNA backbone, with its multiple positive charges creating intense electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged phosphate groups of the DNA backbone, therefore, this compound is a potent multifunctional DNA binding agent with ultra-high affinity, explored as a topoisomerase inhibitor and DNA cross-linker targeting tumor cells.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: [1-[3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-2-oxochromen-7-yl]piperidin-4-yl]-methylazanium, also referred to as SMSM26, is a cationic non-planar coumarin derivative with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, bearing a 1 charge, and is an analogue of [1-[3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-2-oxochromen-7-yl]pyrrolidin-3-yl]-methylazanium; in this molecule, the quaternary ammonium center is connected via a piperidine ring (six-membered), instead of the pyrrolidine ring (five-membered), this change in ring size affects the molecules spatial conformation and stereochemical fit with the target, potentially altering its fluorescence characteristics and biological activity, and it can bind the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics (verified by FP and SAR profiling), displaying 4-fold selectivity over flexible GA-rich RNA, and is used as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging, as well as being investigated as a potential kinase inhibitor or receptor ligand.

RNA information: RNA4 contains the UU internal loop for compound 1 binding, but the GG loop targeted by compound 2 has been mutated to a GC base pair. This mutation significantly reduces the binding affinity of dimeric compound 3, confirming the necessity of the GG loop for high-affinity bivalent binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a mono-benzimidazole Click Chemistry probe with tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module previously identified by the Informa platform to avidly bind a 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop within the Drosha processing site of the pri-miR-96 hairpin precursor, inhibits miR-96 biogenesis and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells with activity that limits its in vivo utility, shows no detectable binding to DNA, and its real-world activity is dedicated to biological orthogonal labeling experiments for target validation and mechanism elucidation.

RNA information: RNA5 contains the GG internal loop for compound 2 binding, but the UU loop for compound 1 has been mutated to a UA base pair. This mutation severely diminishes binding by dimeric compound 3, demonstrating that the UU loop is essential for its high-affinity, bivalent interaction.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targaprimir-96, is the parent, non-covalent small molecule designed to bind selectively to the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96, specifically targeting a UU internal loop within its secondary structure, and by binding to this site, it inhibits the biogenesis of oncogenic mature miR-96, which leads to the upregulation of FOXO1 protein expression and subsequently induces apoptosis in triple-negative breast cancer cells, while showing no significant effect in healthy breast epithelial cells, demonstrating its selective, phenotype-modulating efficacy; this compound is a complex benzimidazole, triazole, and amide derivative, whose structure contains multiple benzimidazole rings, piperazine groups, tert-butyl groups, and a lengthy peptidic mimicry chain, resulting in an extremely high molecular weight and high polar surface area, it presents as a large molecule with extreme complexity and multiple potential hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and its structure is indicative of a potential Anti-cancer Agent or Antiparasitic Agent (Macromolecular Ligand), often designed to interact with DNA or essential biomacromolecular targets (such as protein kinases or enzymes), leveraging its benzimidazole core and polyamine linkers.

RNA information: RNAC is a control RNA hairpin that does not contain the specific internal loop targets for compounds 1 or 2. It features a single-nucleotide change compared to RNA1, which eliminates binding, confirming that the compounds' interactions with other RNAs are specific to the intended motifs.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-methyl-N-pyridin-4-ylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as the final product of a reaction that undergoes nucleophilic substitution and subsequent cyclization under basic conditions (sodium ethoxide) to construct a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and pyridinyl structures. Its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a pyridin-4-ylcarboxamide group at C2, and this complex heterocyclic system also bears amino, methyl, carboxamide, and trifluoromethylphenyl groups indicative of potential biological activity. In terms of biological function, thienopyridine derivatives are known for their broad biological activity, including herbicide safening effects against certain herbicides and strong fluorescence properties.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-phenyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as compound 4, is a fused heterocyclic amide based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold, and it is also a 3-aminothieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative characterized by a phenyl substituent at the 6-position, a structural modification critical for its enhanced potency as a TAR RNA binder. This tricyclic backbone features an amino group at position 3 and a phenyl group at position 6. It is linked via an amide bond to a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl substituent. The molecule is highly lipophilic, thanks to the phenyl group and the crucial trifluoromethyl (CF3) group. The trifluoromethyl group is a classic substituent in medicinal chemistry, used to enhance metabolic stability and transmembrane permeability, while also acting as a strong electron-withdrawing group. This planar, highly lipophilic amidic heterocycle is a prime example of a small molecule drug, often investigated as a non-competitive or competitive inhibitor of signal pathway enzymes like tyrosine kinases, with broad applicability in anti-cancer, immunomodulatory, and anti-inflammatory fields; additionally, it specifically interacts with key nucleobases in the HIV-1 TAR RNA elementG21, U23, and G43demonstrating dual binding affinity for both the bulge region (U23) and the lower stem (G43), a unique interaction profile that establishes it as a promising lead molecule for developing antiviral therapeutics that target RNA-protein interactions.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-[(4-chlorophenyl)methyl]-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 11, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a 4-chlorobenzyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, which features the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, with the carboxamide at the 2-position linked to a 4-chlorobenzyl group; this 4-chlorobenzyl group is a common lipophilic substituent used to improve cell permeability and metabolic stability, and this N-benzyl substituted amide structure is frequently observed in kinase inhibitors, consequently, this molecule is a strong candidate for a kinase inhibitor intended for anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory research, and it also binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key elements for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs with moderate affinity while lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, and its structure represents a typical lead compound in medicinal chemistry used to probe the activity of the thienopyridine scaffold against specific drug targets.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 6,7-dimethoxy-2-piperazin-1-ylquinazolin-4-amine, also referred to as DPQ, is the unsubstituted piperazine amine, serving as the common intermediate or active metabolite for the 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-2-methylpropan-1-one to 6,7-dimethoxy-2-(4-methylsulfonylpiperazin-1-yl)quinazolin-4-amine series; discovered through NMR screening, this compound is a novel small molecule that specifically binds to the (A-A)-U internal loop within the influenza A virus RNA promoter, whose dimethoxy group mediates key interactions with nucleobases in the internal loop such as G13, C22, A11, and A12 thereby widening the major groove, functionally its binding straightens the overall promoter conformation and disrupts adjacent base pairs, this conformational change competitively inhibits the viral RdRp from binding to its promoter site, consequently it effectively inhibits the replication of multiple influenza strains including H1N1, H3N2, and type B virus demonstrating moderate antiviral activity and low cellular cytotoxicity and thus represents a promising lead compound targeting a highly conserved viral RNA element, while in real-world pharmacology, due to its free secondary amine, it is itself an 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist, the secondary amine can be highly protonated at physiological pH, increasing its polarity and potentially leading to higher concentrations in peripheral tissues, and as a core structure, its activity is fundamental to understanding the activity of its acylated derivatives.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-methylacridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 2, is the representative monomeric ligand for this study, containing a 2-carboxamide group and combining two key pharmacophores: the acridine core and the diaminotriazine group, linked by a butylamine chain; it has modest activity, is cell-impermeable, with acridine derivatives being classic DNA intercalators, while the diaminotriazine structure is analogous to the active portion of folic acid antagonists, and this compound is designed as a dual-mechanism drug: the acridine intercalates into DNA and may inhibit topoisomerases, while the triazine component may act as a folate antagonist, consequently, it is studied for potential anticancer and antimalarial activity.

RNA information: (CCUG)₈ is an RNA oligonucleotide modeling the pathogenic expanded repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 2 (DM2). It forms a stable hairpin structure with internal loops and serves as a target for evaluating ligand selectivity. Some small molecules can bind and cleave (CCUG)₈, while others  show no activity, highlighting its role in distinguishing DM2-specific targeting.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[2-aminoethyl-[2-[[2-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)acetyl]amino]ethyl]amino]ethyl]-9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as the imidazole-added acridine-triazine conjugate, is a compound that integrates Acridine (DNA intercalation), Triazine (hydrogen bonding recognition), and an Imidazole functional group, and this imidazole-added acridine-triazine design binds and cleaves both (CUG) and (CCUG) repeats effectively, inhibits transcription moderately, and shows strong bioactivity in cellular and fly models, the imidazole ring, key to molecules like Histamine and Histidine, often serves as a site for proton shuttle and acid-base catalysis in biological systems, this polyfunctional design suggests the compound aims to be a molecule with multiple targeting capabilities, for example, acridine targets nucleic acids, while the imidazole ring may be designed to target specific metalloenzymes (like zinc finger proteins) or G-quadruplex structures, its activity is thus focused on novel anticancer agents or molecular tools for investigating complex enzyme reactions and nucleic acid structures. --

RNA information: The 5′CUG/3′GAC motif is a non-pathogenic RNA internal-loop sequence that forms a 1 × 1 nucleotide U·G wobble when embedded in double-stranded RNA. Although structurally similar to the 5′CUG/3′GUC repeat that drives myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), the single-base change from U to G abolishes high-affinity recognition by MBNL1 and prevents the formation of nuclear foci. Consequently, 5′CUG/3′GAC serves as a “bystander” control in vitro and in cell-based assays to verify that small-molecule ligands or antisense oligonucleotides intended to target the pathogenic 5′CUG/3′GUC repeat do not act through generic U/G- or U/U-loop binding, thereby supporting the development of DM1 therapeutics with improved selectivity.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[6-[2-(3-aminophenyl)-3H-benzimidazol-5-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]aniline, also referred to as Compound H1, is a complex bis-benzimidazole compound (a bis-benzimidazole analog derived from Hoechst 33258) with a structure composed of two benzimidazole units and two meta-aminophenyl units linked covalently, molecular formula C26H21N5; the molecule exhibits a polyheterocyclic structure that is highly conjugated and notably planar, featuring multiple amino and imidazole nitrogen atoms capable of extensive hydrogen bonding, and it retains the core DNA/RNA-binding benzimidazole scaffold of Hoechst 33258, which was rationally designed to target the expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)exp) that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and optimized through chemical-similarity screening to gain higher affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic 5CUG/3GUC RNA motif; in terms of real-world activity, this bis-benzimidazole structure is highly analogous to the structural characteristics of known DNA minor groove binders (e.g., Hoechst dyes), it is designed for high-affinity, specific binding to the DNA minor groove particularly in AT-rich regions, and it also has the ability to disrupt the toxic r(CUG)expMBNL1 complex in vitro, correct MBNL1-dependent splicing defects in DM1 patient-derived cells, disperse nuclear foci without global off-target splicing effects, restore a significant portion of the aberrant splicing of Clcn1 and Serca1 transcripts in the HSALR DM1 mouse model with good tolerance, minimal cytotoxicity and no overt microbiota disturbance, thus establishing itself as a lead small-molecule RNA binder that selectively rescues DM1 molecular phenotypes with potential for further development toward DM1 therapeutics, while it is primarily explored as a lead candidate for novel antitumor or antiviral drugs, acting by interfering with DNA replication, repair, and transcription, and also holds potential as a nuclear DNA stain in biological imaging.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol).
